note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-07-02-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 205.455s but the ffprobed master is 205.009s (0.45s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 13 blocks concatenated with an exact 0.4s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.03-0.05s by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset; final end = ffprobed master duration 205.009s.

phrases.text: Open Employee Schedules from the sidebar.
Four tiles read across the top: who's scheduled this week, how many shifts sit open,
your coverage percentage, and how many sites that covers.
This is your first stop when a manager asks whether the week is staffed.
Now the grid itself.
One row per person, one column per day, Monday through Sunday.
Each cell holds a shift code, a start and end like seven to three, or off for a day off.
Read across a single row and you have that person's whole week.
Above the grid, two panels do the math for you.
One splits the week by time of day: morning, afternoon, evening.
The other rates coverage, how much is covered, at risk, or still open.
You can spot a thin day before you ever open a row.
Switch to Time Clock.
Schedules are the plan.
This is what actually happened.
The tile to watch is On Clock Now, your live count of everyone punched in across the network,
right this second.
Run your eye down the clock-out column.
Most rows show a time in and a time out.
One shows a dash.
That dash isn't missing data.
It means the person is still on the clock, and their hours keep climbing until they punch out.
Off to the side, the punch-status panel.
It splits today into on the clock, clocked out, and no show.
No show is the one to scan for: scheduled staff who never punched in.
That's a call worth making before the shift falls short.
Open Employee Roles.
A role is a job's permission set, what that person is allowed to touch.
The tiles count your total roles, how many are active,
and how many sit at manager or admin level.
The list names each role and, in the last column, its access level.
Administrator has full reach.
A cashier gets the point of sale and little else.
When someone can't do something they expect to, this column is usually the reason.
The access-levels panel sums it up.
Most of your staff sit at the low tier, and that's by design.
Far more cashiers and attendants than managers.
The panel beside it ranks roles by how many people hold each one.
Last page, User Groups.
A group is a named set of people, and it's a different idea from a role.
A role is about permissions.
A group is about who belongs together.
Each group lists its description, its member count, and its locations.
Some cover a single site, like the M-L-A-S staff group.
Others span every location, like all cashiers.
The locations column tells you a group's reach at a glance.
One thing that isn't obvious on screen.
Groups are how Maintenance work gets assigned.
Route an issue to the detail team, and it reaches everyone in that group.
So hold the split in your head: roles decide what a person can do,
groups decide who the work lands on.
Four pages, four questions.
Schedules, for who works which days.
Time Clock, for who's punched in right now.
Roles, for what someone's allowed to do.
Groups, for who belongs to which team.
Every one is a list you read, never a screen you edit.
Trust the tiles for the live count, read the rows for the detail,
and leave the punches and schedules alone.
